title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee met on May 11, 2026, approving several key items including a recycled asphalt crushing contract with King Gravel for $126,900, a resolution supporting state transportation funding, and construction service proposals for two road projects. The committee also discussed ongoing infrastructure repairs, grant funding successes totaling over $9 million in recent years, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines starting at $100 per day.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permit
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual reports
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkkey Avenue duplex
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported that the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project, which will likely move to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. Michael noted the village has secured over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for pavement depression over a sanitary sewer trench, and ongoing repairs needed on Schofield Avenue and Fuller Street including manhole adjustments.

Staff reported on seasonal activities including removal of road limits, mailbox and sign repairs, spring curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Dewatering operations were conducted near Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week due to high water levels. Storm damage cleanup was performed after an event off East Nick. Culvert failures on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch culvert. Equipment maintenance included removing plow equipment, servicing mowers, and ordering a new Mac tandem axle chassis.

Josh reported on water service repairs at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance, and ongoing private well sampling. On the sanitary side, lift stations experienced significant activity due to high groundwater, with clear water infiltration being a major issue. April data showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. The committee discussed that residents illegally connecting sump pumps to sanitary sewers contributes to this problem.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing operating revenue of approximately $3 million and net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. Cash reserves stand at $6.8 million. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term funding solutions for the state transportation fund. He noted that while the village receives grant funding for one-time costs, recurring annual funding for street maintenance and snow plowing remains inadequate, requiring subsidization from general tax dollars. The committee voted unanimously to recommend the village board adopt the resolution.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled material at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23 per ton for an estimated 30,000 tons, totaling $126,900. Michael demonstrated this saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to purchasing material, saving about $130,000 on the three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on fines for residents illegally discharging sump pumps into sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. After discussion distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations, the committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines starting at $100 per day. Staff will wait for the INI study results to identify problem areas before sending violation notices. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained that a builder wants to construct a duplex on lot one, which lacks frontage on a water main. Staff proposed allowing a temporary long lateral connection until Radkkey Avenue reconstruction, at which time the owner would pay assessment for main extension. The committee amended the agreement to specify reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for construction of Military Road from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share for water and sanitary sewer work is $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For the Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910; staff recommended MSA. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730; staff recommended Clark Dietz for the additional $3,000 due to having full-time experienced staff rather than a summer intern. Both recommendations were approved unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day starting fine
Recommend approval of water/sewer connection agreement for Radkkey Avenue duplex with reconstruction deadline of 2029
Recommend approval of Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860 Weston share
Award MSA construction services for Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project ($37,315)
Award Clark Dietz construction services for Bladel Avenue project ($73,730)
Schedule special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue project on May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration treatment costs for next meeting
Consider adding street lighting to Schofield Avenue between Birch Street and Camp Phillips Road during 2030 reconstruction

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission also heard that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: The commission elected three officers unanimously. Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair. Cushing was nominated by a commissioner, seconded by Jackson, and elected as Vice Chair. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary. All votes passed without opposition.
The minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) represented the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year. He announced that the competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. A commissioner noted the application showed 20 fewer hotel rooms than last year and requested better tracking of hotel room usage from the Holiday Inn Express where pilots stay. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request for better room tracking data in their post-event report. Motion passed unanimously, seconded by Jackson.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees. The candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese. This was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s. Renee noted the candidates were appreciative of the community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow Balloonfest to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with all commissioners present voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Breath and communication disclosure and recusal
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Unfinished business - Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to avoid conflicts with a 36-inch gas pipeline running through the property. Chief Jeremy explained the building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond current staffing of 40 officers. An alternate storage shed is proposed but may be built later depending on bid costs. Discussion included future expansion possibilities to the north, landscaping compliance with current code, and drainage considerations for shed placement. Motion to approve was made and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Jennifer presented a new inspection program requiring two annual inspections of salt storage facilities within wellhead protection areas - one in warm weather focusing on structural integrity and one in winter focusing on operations. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT requirements was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Michael noted the village's wells are shallow (90-110 ft) with sandy soil making contamination risk higher. Motion to approve with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented was made and passed unanimously without discussion.
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps, site plans, and building permits were approved unanimously without discussion.

Jennifer announced the first joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send calendar invites. Discussion included potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with commissioners requesting to see a presentation from Ehlers on residential TIF qualifications, possibly incorporated into the comprehensive plan process.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online participation. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, citing the Green Heck Turner building as an example of excessive requirements. He also requested that the salt storage shed inspection program include annual reporting on inspection results given there are not many facilities in wellhead protection zones.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for implementation within wellhead protection district
Staff to report back to Plan Commission and Public Works committee on inspection program results next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
Joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8th, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, receiving educational presentations on four departments: Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes and heard detailed overviews of each department's mission, structure, budget, and current initiatives. No substantive policy votes were taken beyond the minutes approval.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (noting Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg remote)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations functions required by statute - no items this meeting
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, missions and responsibilities

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavalli moved to approve the minutes, Vice Chair Rosenberg seconded. Motion carried unanimously with voice vote.

Administrator Lance Leonard provided an overview of section 2.04.5 of county ordinances governing the committee's jurisdiction. He explained the committee oversees Veteran Services, Social Services, and Health Department directly, plus affiliated entities North Central Health Care and Aging & Disability Resource Center of Central Wisconsin. He outlined various statutory boards that report to this committee including Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect from environmental hazards. She highlighted over 1,200 licenses overseen, infectious disease monitoring including recent tick advisory, Nurse Family Partnership program graduating first cohort of two-year-olds, and a new homeless system response position. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE staff across environmental health, nursing, and community health improvement teams. Administrator Leonard noted about half their $5 million budget is tax levy, with significant intergovernmental grant revenue.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. The department of nearly 140 FTE handles child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program. The department has a $22 million total budget with about $7 million in tax levy. Leonard noted they expect restructuring requests for some units due to increased need.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported his department increased to 3.0 FTE this year. In 2025, customer interactions jumped from 5,900 to 8,149, and veterans received $2.6 million in retroactive payments plus $44 million annually in benefits. Key initiatives include housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues and suicide prevention efforts - noting Marathon County had 30 suicides in 2025, seven being veterans. Galindo stated his team averages 20-30 contacts daily each and he acknowledged staffing constraints but noted they're meeting demands.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the Aging & Disability Resource Center serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties. The organization of about 60 FTE plus 385-400 volunteers provides Meals on Wheels, Medicare counseling, falls prevention, and serves as entry point to Family Care and IRIS programs. He noted nutrition program costs increased 40-45% over four years with federal funding flat since 2024. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135. They hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024.

actionItems: Schedule North Central Healthcare for separate full presentation before committee
Next meeting confirmed as June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion to future meetings
Committee members to email suggestions for future agenda items to Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing concerns about the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and approved license renewals for the upcoming licensing term.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, Pledge of Allegiance, Roll Call
Public Comments - Jim Pitel regarding engineering consultants and committee appointments
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fee amendments
Approval of meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Work product transmittals - building permits, budget status, code enforcement
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances - mobile food vendor, standing committees, nuisances, e-bikes, transportation funding resolution
Committee appointments and new business items
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fee Amendment
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel commented during public hearing that the village lacks detailed data on park maintenance costs. Trustee Olsen asked about fuel costs and when fees were last adjusted (2022). Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passed unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 amending the fee schedule was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passed unanimously.
Minutes from April 13, 2026 special board meeting and April 21, 2026 board meeting were approved. Motion by Zagami, second by Beck, passed unanimously.

Trustees provided brief updates from their committee assignments. Parks reported Yellow Banks mat installation and tree removal from the river. Public Works noted upcoming contracts for recycling and project inspections. Tourism awarded grants including $10,000 to Wausau Mum Festival, $5,000 to Mountain Bay Cup, and $2,500 to GG's. Finance discussed election costs and parking regulations. MPD Joint Finance Committee voted to disperse funds back to the three communities.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional fire inspection sticker system with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, Riverside, and Marathon County. Once a food truck is inspected by one participating department, others accept the sticker. Motion by Lope, second by Zagami, passed unanimously.

Board discussed differences between e-bikes and e-motos (which can go 52 mph). The ordinance amends chapter 82 traffic and vehicles article 3 bicycles. Motion by Zagami, second by Clark, passed unanimously.

Board debated striking language requiring health, safety, or welfare justification for remote attendance. Trustee Beck moved to amend by removing sentences requiring justification reasons, keeping only requirements to notify 12 hours in advance, be on camera, and not participate in closed session. Amendment motion by Beck, second by Clark, passed with two no votes (Hermling and Olsen). Original motion as amended by Zagami, second by Hermling, passed with same two no votes.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lope, passed unanimously. Board agreed to include committee/commission applications in future packets.

discussions.item: Secondhand Dealer License - Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC
discussions.body: Chief Hunt explained the license is required for businesses buying/selling precious metals and large dollar transactions, with items logged on a police-accessible database. Motion by Hermling, second by Clark, amended to require satisfactory background check and receipt of occupancy permit since building is under construction. Passed unanimously as amended.

Board discussed concerns about the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park after the previous Camp Phillips/Scoffield site was sold for development. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson. Trustee Olsen and others debated parking issues. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor stalls and vendors appreciate having vehicles with them. Mont Miller Park was discussed but rejected due to location being too remote. Trustee Beck questioned costs but was assured the market operates at a surplus through vendor fees and grants for food assistance programs. Discussion only - no action taken.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52 Street spoke during general public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced at the April Fire Department meeting as a Safer board member before being officially appointed by the board. He also criticized the lack of detailed Kennedy Park information in the packet. During the park impact fee public hearing, Pitel commented that the village lacks specific data on park maintenance costs to justify the fee amounts.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with ordinance 26-010 and resolution 2026-015
Comprehensive Plan Steering Committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement with Town of Ringle for building inspection assistance
Electronics drive rescheduled to June 6th
Weston Avenue speed limit study to return to board once complete
Engineering technician position continues to be advertised
Remote meeting attendance policy updated with amended language
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Committee/commission applications to be included in future board packets
Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC license approved pending background check and occupancy permit

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee at-large positions, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris following recent severe weather that devastated parts of the county, particularly the Town of Ringle.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Roll call (technical difficulties with meeting manager system)
Committee appointments - Chair remarks on leadership and appointments
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Consent agenda items C10 through C14A
Resolution 24-26 - Out of home placement reserve increase

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs outlined goals for committee appointments including increasing diversity of perspectives, developing new supervisors as leaders, and honoring supervisor preferences. He noted 34% of the board is in first or second terms. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, included both county board chair and administrator appointments. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained her nomination of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's youngest and oldest residents. Paper ballots were used and Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented the emergency resolution following severe weather that devastated Ringle and other communities. The state waived $13.10 per ton, Ringle waived $2.33 per ton, and the county proposed reducing fees to $36.94 per ton (commercial rate minus waivers) to cover costs. Special public comment was allowed for Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, who requested the county wave all fees for affected residents and extend relief beyond 60 days. The board unanimously suspended rules to consider the resolution and then unanimously approved it. Administrator Leonard committed to exploring partnerships with the Community Foundation to help residents with unreimbursed costs and developing a long-term policy within six months.
Items C10 through C14A were approved unanimously on motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck.

discussions.item: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase (Resolution 24-26)
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

publicComment: Special dispensation was granted for two members of the public to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty, a Ringle resident, asked the county to completely wave its fees, noting the state, DNR, and Town of Ringle had waived their portions. She stated businesses have donated up to $40,000 in cleanup costs and asked for six months of relief. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents with demolished homes, requesting fee waivers for underinsured homeowners and noting some insurance adjusters haven't yet arrived. He mentioned approximately 44 homes were affected.
actionItems: Reduced landfill tipping fees approved for disaster-related debris through June 17, 2025 (duration of state emergency declaration)
Administrator Leonard to work with Community Foundation on mechanism to reimburse residents for out-of-pocket disposal costs not covered by insurance
Solid Waste Management Board directed to develop long-term policy for handling similar disaster situations within six months
Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Board to revisit tipping fee relief timeline at May meeting if extension is needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and amended a resolution to waive only septic system reconnection fees (not broader zoning fees) for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado before forwarding it to the county board. The meeting also featured department overviews from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview presentation
Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview presentation

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes with Supervisor Drebeck seconding. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for, against, or as interested. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval; Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from conservancy recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Lever asked about the conservancy designation and potential loss of natural areas. Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and the rezone would make these conforming uses rather than non-conforming. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve; Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather damage
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic system fees for properties damaged by the April 17 tornado affecting towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck questioned fairness to those affected by non-declared disasters and noted the recent fee increase for septic systems. After debate, Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, not other zoning or new system fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment passed. The amended resolution then passed on a non-unanimous vote to forward to the county board.

Director Dave Hagenbusher presented on the department's operations, explaining the landfill accepts about 1,000 tons daily with 11 FTE staff. He discussed the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy generating royalties for the county, significant capital expenses of $4-6 million for expansions and closures, and the requirement by 2032 to have on-site leachate treatment due to PFAS concerns. The department's environmental loan application for treatment facilities was deemed incomplete and will be resubmitted for 2027 funding.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie. The department operates both county and city of Wausau park systems with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees. Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service, with Joe Tucker selected as the new county forest administrator.

actionItems: Forward amended resolution to county board to waive only septic system reconnect fees ($160) for properties damaged by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Forward James Offer/David Track rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) to county board for approval
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) to county board for approval
Track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
CPZ to resubmit environmental loan application for leachate treatment facility by October 2025 for 2027 funding
Schedule summer educational meeting on conservation topics with Conservation Director Kirsty Heiden

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved signage for Yellow Banks kayak launches, deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and the Oaklair River Trail graffiti issues. The meeting featured public comment criticizing both the dog day proposal and the transparency of Kennedy Park fundraising efforts.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes were approved with a motion and second. The motion carried with all in favor and no opposition.

Staff presented a timeline document showing the Kennedy Park master plan process from its approval in November 2023 through the May 9, 2026 ribbon cutting. Director Oster explained that the Friends of Kennedy Park are currently fundraising for lights on the upper field, with approximately $84-85,000 currently at the community foundation toward a $117,000 goal. Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital funds, and Oster confirmed no village capital dollars are currently earmarked for Kennedy Park projects. Staff noted the Friends group plans a public check presentation at their first tournament in June. Committee members discussed that master plans are guiding documents subject to change and that the Friends group deserves grace as volunteers.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and a PDF showing the location of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during logging equipment access. Dan explained the mat installation process and showed that some mats remain for possible future trail projects.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the next meeting because Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. The chair noted that since Katrina had requested this agenda item, she should have the opportunity to speak on it. The resident who spoke against the dog day proposal during public comment said he may return next month.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches on the trail, noting the pressure washer cannot access the area due to steep ditches and narrow bridges. Discussion included possible anti-vandalism coatings and organizing volunteer work days for trail maintenance. Committee member stated she would participate and suggested community volunteers might help. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate options.

Dan presented version seven of signage for kayak launches along the river, explaining that signs would be installed at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks in the Village of Weston, plus locations in the City of Schofield. The signs are funded through Marathon County Parks Foundation grants. Dan noted the Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve the signs and forward to the village board was made and seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

Staff mentioned receiving an email requesting participation in placing food allergy awareness signs at playgrounds. The committee agreed to bring this back for future discussion to allow other members and the public to weigh in.

publicComment: Two public comments were offered. Andy from 6504 [street inaudible] spoke against allowing dogs at the aquatic center, stating animals should not be allowed in a public pool and belong in rivers and lakes. Jim Pensan (appearing online as 'Vern M') from 5002 [street] Weston criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation, and questioned the Kennedy Park funding report, calling the $8,700 figure for equipment 'too coincidental' and demanding to know where grant money and glow event proceeds are. He characterized the Friends of Kennedy Park financial reporting as 'convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Yellow Banks kayak launch signs approved and forwarded to village board for final approval
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate graffiti removal options and possible anti-vandalism coatings for Oaklair River Trail
Staff to bring food allergy awareness signage proposal back for future discussion
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11th, 5-7 p.m.

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April budget and financial reports. The meeting featured an educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam Akey about her department's operations, covering elections, licensing, tax collection, and the village newsletter. Committee member Evan Alborn announced his resignation as he is moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. Motion carried with unanimous voice vote.

A committee member asked about the status of TIF bond payoff. Finance Director Jessica explained they are anticipating October/November for final payoff, noting the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds. She confirmed the numbers still look good and the village will be able to pay off the debt. The bonds are due October 1st.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam Akey presented an overview of her department, which now has 2.5 staff members down from 4 full-time previously. She explained election administration including the use of Badger Books (electronic poll books at $11,000 for 4 units), noting they handled the 2024 presidential election with almost 800 registrations and over 600 in-person absentee voters on the first day. She discussed plans to increase chief inspector pay from approximately $13 to $15 per hour to match county averages. Akey explained that pet licensing was brought back in-house from third-party Pet Data, collecting $17,480 with $8,067 retained by the village. The newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing to reach 5,089 postal patrons.

Akey explained the recent increase in pet licensing fees (from $10 to $20 for neutered animals) was prompted by Marathon County raising fees charged to municipalities to cover their $100,000 annual payment to the Humane Society, which they were collecting only $65,000 for previously.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well. She has been on-site the last three Saturdays from 6 AM for 6-7 hours helping vendors. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft vendors, and some greenhouse produce vendors. A committee member expressed concern about parking on both sides of Alderson and Newport streets. Staff clarified parking is only allowed on the west side of Alderson, with the east side still signed no parking.
Evan Alborn announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though remaining in the metro area. He thanked the committee for the opportunity to serve.

actionItems: CDA will need to pass resolution to call TIF bonds, anticipated this summer with October 1st bond due date
Chief inspector pay increase to $15/hour to be proposed for next election term
Seeking citizens to fill open committee spots - multiple vacancies exist
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th and took several significant actions including recommending approval of amendments to the nuisance ordinance, an e-bike ordinance prohibiting e-motos on roadways and sidewalks, a mobile food vendor fire inspection program, address corrections for E911 compliance, and a raze order for an abandoned structure. The committee also discussed but tabled overnight parking regulations for further staff research.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Mobile food vendor fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Jim Senalt addressed the committee with concerns about the new farmers market location at a park, stating it was 'a terrible execution' and predicting the grass would be destroyed. He questioned ADA compliance for handicap parking on grass rather than asphalt, asked about the origin of fire inspections for food trucks, and raised concerns about code enforcement cases that have been pending for years without visible progress.

Code Enforcement reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the enforcement roll after the property sold at auction and the new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work summer zoning violations for Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield. Reports were acknowledged by motion from Chwanka, seconded by Olsen, passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including corrected tree limb heights of 14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways. Olsen made a motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees, seconded by Campkie, passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion focused on e-motos (electric dirt bikes). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting e-motos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. The committee debated whether to include electric scooters, with member Mike noting many middle school kids use them for transportation. Chief Hunt suggested adding 'with exemption to kids play scooters' for officer discretion. Olsen moved to recommend approval with the addition prohibiting e-motos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property. Seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour no-parking window (4am-4pm) is far outside other municipalities' 3.5-5 hour windows. Michael (Public Works) noted plowing doesn't typically reach residential streets until 7-8am, making overnight restrictions less useful. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight parking restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. The committee decided to table the item until July, directing Michael to identify 6-8 specific problem areas that could be signed individually.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their property and DC Everest school parking lot. Michael estimated $2,000 for a basic crosswalk, $25,000-$30,000 for a lighted system. The committee was reluctant to set precedent of paying for requested crosswalks. Olsen moved to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering installation costs. Seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously.

Chief Hunt explained a new multi-municipality fire inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston. One inspection with a sticker is valid across all participating municipalities. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Chief noted a recent inspection failed due to improper galvanized steel LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Chwanka moved to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkie, passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue apartments. Chief Hunt noted an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski with eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure that has been vacant since 2010-2011. A previous fire department training burn was cancelled due to unsafe conditions. The current owners housed chickens inside the structure. Owners are current on taxes and preferred the raze approach. Olsen moved to recommend authorization, seconded by Campkie, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment, criticizing the new farmers market location as 'a terrible execution' that would destroy park grass. He questioned ADA compliance for handicap parking on grass, asked about the origin of food truck fire inspection requirements, and expressed concern about long-pending code enforcement cases with no visible progress.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 Nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance with e-moto prohibition to Board of Trustees
Michael to research specific problem areas for overnight parking restrictions and return in July
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church about paying for crosswalk installation
Forward Ordinance 26-8 (mobile food vendor fire inspections) for board approval
Recommend address corrections to Board of Trustees
Recommend raze order authorization for 4104 Shore Avenue to Board of Trustees
Staff to investigate property condition at Sternberg and South Timber
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 pm

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
